title: practical english usage - Part 294
content:
It's very hot at the centre of the earth.

At is used to talk about position at a point.

384 at,   on   and   in:  place I asked Jack if I could go home. (I wanted to go home myself.) I asked Jack to go home. (I wanted Jack to go home.) Note the difference between these two sentences: I asked for the package to he sent to my home address.

I asked for the children to have extra milk.

ask + for + object + infinitive I asked Jack to go home. (= I told Jack I would like him to go home.) ask + object + infinitive I asked to go home. (= I asked permission to go home.) ask + infinitive We   can use infinitive structures after ask (►  97-98).

2  infinitive       structures vocabulary  •  384   at, on and in: place H She lives in a room on the third floor.

We   use on for the number of the floor.

She lives in Albert Street.

We use in (AmE on) if we  just give the name of the street.

Are you still at the same address? She lives at 73 Albert Street.

We   generally use at to talk about addresses.

What time do we arrive in New York?

He arrives at the airport at 15.30. (     H e arrives-to-the-airport...) We   generally use at (not to) after arrive; in is used before very large places.

He fell into the river when he was getting out of his canoe.

She came in a taxi.

But we  use in and out (of) to talk about cars and small private planes and boats.

I'll go down to the shop on my bike.

It took five days to cross the Atlantic on the Queen Elizabeth.

We're travelling on flight 604.

He's arriving on the 3.15 train, (     . .. in/with-the5 train.) There's no room on the bus; let's get off again.

planes and boats), as well as (motor)cycles and horses.

We   use on (and off) to talk about travel using public transport (buses, trains, 4 public     transport He lived in the desert for three years.

She grew up in Swaziland.    I last saw her in Times Square.

I don't think he's in his office.  Let's go for a walk in the woods.

(when something is surrounded on all sides).

In is used for position inside large areas, and in three-dimensional space 3 in Bowness is on Lake Windermere. Southend-on-Sea On  is also used for position by a lake or sea.

There aren't many apples on the tree this year.

Why do you wear that ring on your first finger?

On  can mean 'attached to*.

There's a big spider on the ceiling.

That picture would look better on the other wall.

Hurry up - supper's on the table!

On  is used for position on a surface.

Who's the good-looking boy in the sixth row?

There's a misprint in line 6 on page 22.

But in is used for the position of things which  form part of the line.

Stratford is on the river Avon.

His house is on the way from Aberdeen to Dundee.

On  is used to talk about position on a line (for example a road or a river).

Word  Problems from A  to Z      Section 31 vocabulary •  385  at/in and to Could you shout to Jake and tell him  it's breakfast time?

Please do not throw food to the animals.

Throw to, shout to and point to are used when there is no idea of attack.

In my dream, everybody was pointing at me and laughing.

You don't need to shout at me.

Stop throwing stones at the cat, darling.

If you can't laugh at yourself who can you laugh at?

It's a strange feeling to have somebody shoot at you.

Common examples are shoot, laugh, throw, shout and point.

At is also used after some verbs referring to attacks or aggressive behaviour.

Why are you looking at her like that? Because she smiled at me.

communication. Common examples are look, smile, wave, frown.

After some verbs, at is used with the 'target' of a perception or non-verbal 3  targets not I went to see my father in Canada. (     I went to see my father to Canada.) -   I went to Canada to see my father.

Let's go and have coffee at Marcel's. (     Let's go and have coffee to Marcel's.) - Let's go to Marcel's for coffee.

we  usually use at/in before the place. Compare: If we  mention the purpose of a movement before we  mention the destination, 2 expressions         of  purpose I go to Canada to see him  whenever I can.

- My father lives in Canada.
